name: Rounding the Altos del Zancado - circular from Aliaga
name_original: Rondeando los Altos del Zancado - circular desde Aliaga
region_intro: Road cycling around Aliaga is characterized by diverse and rugged terrain, featuring significant mountain ranges such as the Sierra de San Just and Sierra del Pobo. The region includes dramatic geological formations like the Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges, offering routes with substantial elevation gains. Cyclists will encounter varied landscapes, from remote highlands to rolling hills, with mostly paved surfaces. Best road cycling routes around Aliaga The most popular road cycling route is Rounding the Altos del Zancado - circular from Aliaga, a 32.6 miles (52.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 58 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers scenic views across rolling hills. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Gudar Pass – Alcalá de la Selva loop from Aliaga, a difficult 61.1 miles (98.4 km) path. This route traverses mountain ranges, providing challenging climbs and expansive vistas. Local road cyclists also love the Gorge of the Río de la Val – Puerto de El Esquinazo loop from Aliaga, a 59.2 miles (95.3 km) trail leading through dramatic geological formations, often completed in about 4 hours 26 minutes. Road cycling around Aliaga is defined by mountain ranges, deep gorges, and remote highlands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to those requiring very good fitness. The routes in Aliaga are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 9 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Aliaga's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Aliaga?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes available around Aliaga, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. The komoot community has rated these routes highly, with an average score of 5.0 stars.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Aliaga?
A: Road cycling in Aliaga features diverse and rugged terrain, including significant mountain ranges like the Sierra de San Just and Sierra del Pobo. You'll encounter dramatic geological formations such as the Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges, along with remote highlands and rolling hills. While most surfaces are paved, some segments may be unpaved and challenging, requiring good fitness.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or less experienced road cyclists?
A: While many routes in Aliaga are classified as difficult due to substantial elevation gains, there are also moderate options available. For example, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961419999/rounding-the-altos-del-zancado-circular-from-aliaga" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rounding the Altos del Zancado - circular from Aliaga&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 32.6-mile route offering scenic views across rolling hills, making it a good choice for those seeking a less strenuous ride.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Experienced road cyclists will find plenty of challenging routes in Aliaga. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gudar Pass – Alcalá de la Selva loop from Aliaga&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 61.1-mile path traversing mountain ranges with significant climbs. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143712" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorge of the Río de la Val – Puerto de El Esquinazo loop from Aliaga&lt;/a&gt;, a 59.2-mile trail through dramatic geological formations.

Q: What natural attractions or viewpoints can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Aliaga's road cycling routes offer access to impressive natural features and viewpoints. You can encounter dramatic gorges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5248074" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valloré Footbridges&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2088068" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Órganos de Montoro Pass&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1637345" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Majalinos&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247947" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Pitarque River&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3793681" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall in Barranco de la Hoz Mala on the Guadalope River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical landmarks accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, the region around Aliaga integrates cultural and historical points of interest into its cycling experience. The historic Camino de los Pilones is part of a touring route, and you can find the historic Ermita de Sant Just, built in 1758, accessible by bike. The 'Monumento The Silent Route' on the A-1702 highway is also a notable landmark for cyclists.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Aliaga?
A: The diverse terrain of Aliaga, with its mountain ranges and gorges, suggests that spring and autumn would likely offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the extreme heat of summer and potential winter conditions at higher elevations. Always check local weather forecasts before planning your ride.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Aliaga?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Aliaga are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961419999/rounding-the-altos-del-zancado-circular-from-aliaga" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rounding the Altos del Zancado - circular from Aliaga&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gudar Pass – Alcalá de la Selva loop from Aliaga&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20144093" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Cabigordo top of the col sign. – Teruel Cathedral Square loop from Aliaga&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Aliaga?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 9 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse and rugged terrain, the challenging climbs, and the spectacular views across mountain ranges and dramatic geological formations like the gorges. The variety in route difficulty also ensures options for different skill levels.

Q: Are there options for parking near the road cycling routes in Aliaga?
A: While specific parking locations are not detailed for every route, Aliaga is a destination for outdoor activities, and routes often start from the town itself or nearby villages. It is generally advisable to check the starting points of individual routes for available parking facilities.

Q: Can I find amenities like cafes or accommodation suitable for cyclists in Aliaga?
A: Aliaga is a destination for outdoor enthusiasts, and while specific cyclist-friendly amenities are not listed in detail, it is reasonable to expect local cafes, pubs, and accommodation options in and around the town that cater to visitors. It's always a good idea to check in advance for services along your chosen route.
tips: Ascent to Camarillas from Aliaga. First section bearable and a little harder in the middle. Short, with good views. Be careful with the asphalt, there is a lot of loose gravel and some holes due to the cold in winter.
Camarillas is a town located in the province of Teruel, under the Sierra de Lastra. One of its characteristics is that it still preserves an old aqueduct that continues to function today.

The Sanctuary of the Virgen del Campo in Camarillas is an architectural complex in which the old hermitage, the new hermitage and several secondary constructions are concentrated around an open courtyard with a rectangular floor plan.

The old hermitage dates back to the 14th century, is Gothic in style and opens onto the courtyard through a two-story Renaissance porch built entirely of ashlar stone, attached to the south wall of the hermitage. The hermitage, which has stone masonry, has a very simple layout, with a polygonal apse and a single nave covered with ribbed vaults. The apse inside is separated from the nave by a partition wall and a brick Mudejar tower is attached to the outside, built in the 16th century.

The new hermitage is a building from the 18th century, larger than the previous one and also built in masonry and ashlar. It consists of three naves with four sections, a transept covered with a dome and a straight chancel. On the outside, its classicist façade stands out and on the inside there are several mural paintings in the Baroque style.

Among the secondary constructions of a popular nature, the so-called "House of the Hermit" from the 16th century stands out.
Fountain at the Mezquita de Jarque exit towards Son Del Puerto. Always with plenty of water.
